---
title: "Syllabus"
description: "Syllabus — FYS: Progress"
---

```{=html}
<!-- ── Page Header ────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<header style="padding-block: var(--sp-2xl); border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);">
  <div class="container--text">
    <p class="hero__eyebrow">AS.001.219 · Fall 2026</p>
    <h1>Syllabus</h1>
    <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 1rem; color: var(--ink-mid); font-style: italic; margin: calc(var(--sp-sm) * -1) 0 var(--sp-lg);">FYS: <em>Progress</em> — Why now is the best time to be alive (and how we could lose it)</p>
    <p class="lead">
      Twenty-six sessions across six modules. Tuesdays and Thursdays, 10:30–11:45 AM.
      First class: September 1. Last class: December 10.
    </p>
  </div>
</header>

<!-- ── Course Information ─────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section>
  <div class="container">
    <h2 class="mb-lg">Course information</h2>
    <div class="info-grid">
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Instructor</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">Simon D. Halliday</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Email</div>
        <div class="info-block__value"><a href="mailto:simon.halliday@jhu.edu">simon.halliday@jhu.edu</a></div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Office hours</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">TBD — see Canvas</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Meeting time</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">Tue &amp; Thu, 10:30–11:45 AM</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Location</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">TBD — Homewood Campus</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Canvas</div>
        <div class="info-block__value"><a href="https://canvas.jhu.edu">canvas.jhu.edu</a></div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Grading ────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section style="background: var(--bg-alt);">
  <div class="container--text">
    <h2>Grades</h2>

    <table style="width:100%; border-collapse:collapse; font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.88rem; margin-top: var(--sp-lg);">
      <thead>
        <tr style="border-bottom: 2px solid var(--accent);">
          <th style="text-align:left; padding: 8px 12px 8px 0; color: var(--ink-mid); font-weight:600; letter-spacing:0.04em; text-transform:uppercase; font-size:0.72rem;">Assignment</th>
          <th style="text-align:right; padding: 8px 0 8px 12px; color: var(--ink-mid); font-weight:600; letter-spacing:0.04em; text-transform:uppercase; font-size:0.72rem;">Weight</th>
        </tr>
      </thead>
      <tbody>
        <tr style="border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);">
          <td style="padding: 10px 12px 10px 0;">Baltimore Progress Video <span style="color:var(--ink-light); font-size:0.82em;">(group)</span></td>
          <td style="text-align:right; padding: 10px 0 10px 12px; color:var(--accent); font-weight:600;">30%</td>
        </tr>
        <tr style="border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);">
          <td style="padding: 10px 12px 10px 0;">Progress Data Project <span style="color:var(--ink-light); font-size:0.82em;">(individual)</span></td>
          <td style="text-align:right; padding: 10px 0 10px 12px; color:var(--accent); font-weight:600;">30%</td>
        </tr>
        <tr style="border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);">
          <td style="padding: 10px 12px 10px 0;">In-class reading responses <span style="color:var(--ink-light); font-size:0.82em;">(handwritten, start of class)</span></td>
          <td style="text-align:right; padding: 10px 0 10px 12px; color:var(--accent); font-weight:600;">15%</td>
        </tr>
        <tr style="border-bottom: 1px solid var(--rule);">
          <td style="padding: 10px 12px 10px 0;">Participation &amp; discussion leadership</td>
          <td style="text-align:right; padding: 10px 0 10px 12px; color:var(--accent); font-weight:600;">15%</td>
        </tr>
        <tr>
          <td style="padding: 10px 12px 10px 0;">Opening &amp; closing reflection</td>
          <td style="text-align:right; padding: 10px 0 10px 12px; color:var(--accent); font-weight:600;">10%</td>
        </tr>
      </tbody>
    </table>

    <p class="small text-mid mt-md">
      Full descriptions of each assignment are on the <a href="assignments.html">Assignments page</a>.
      AI use is expected and encouraged — each assignment includes an AI log component.
    </p>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Academic Calendar Notes ───────────────────────────────── -->
<section>
  <div class="container--text">
    <h2>Key dates</h2>
    <div class="info-grid mt-lg" style="grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(200px, 1fr));">
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">First class</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">Tuesday, Sep 1</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Add/drop deadline</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">Monday, Sep 11 (noon)</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Last day to drop</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">Monday, Oct 12</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Thanksgiving week</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">Nov 24 &amp; Nov 26 — no classes</div>
      </div>
      <div class="info-block">
        <div class="info-block__label">Last class</div>
        <div class="info-block__value">Thursday, Dec 10</div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Module 1 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section id="module-1" style="background: var(--bg-alt);">
  <div class="container">
    <div class="syllabus-module">
      <div class="syllabus-module__header">
        <span class="syllabus-module__num">Module 1</span>
        <h2 class="syllabus-module__title">What Is Progress?</h2>
      </div>
      <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--ink-mid); margin-bottom: var(--sp-lg); max-width: 600px;">
        We begin with the data. How much has the world actually improved, and how do we know?
        We then ask what we should even be measuring — and whose definition of progress counts.
      </p>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Sep 1</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title"><a href="session-notes/session01-introduction.html">Session 1 · Introduction: Why Progress?</a></div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Course overview. Opening reflection: "What do you think the world's biggest unsolved problem is, and why?" No assigned reading for today. Baltimore Video groups assigned; sites chosen by Thursday.
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Steven Pinker, <em>Enlightenment Now</em>, Chapter 4: "Progressophobia" (2018) — why do intellectuals and the media systematically resist evidence that the world is getting better? Pinker traces the cultural and psychological roots of progress-denial. Pairs well with Rosling.</li>
            </ul></div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">Opening reflection due</span><span class="tag tag--accent">Video groups assigned</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Sep 3</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title"><a href="session-notes/session02-chimpanzee-test.html">Session 2 · How Much Has the World Improved? Data and Instincts</a></div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Baltimore Video sites confirmed by end of this week; filming begins.
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Hans Rosling, <em>Factfulness</em>, Introduction + Chapter 1 ("The Gap Instinct") — why experts score worse than chimpanzees on basic facts about the world, and why the developing/developed binary is an outdated map of reality.</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span><span class="tag tag--accent">Video sites confirmed</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Sep 8</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title"><a href="session-notes/session03-progress-and-freedom.html">Session 3 · Progress Getting Better — And What That Means</a></div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Hans Rosling, <em>Factfulness</em>, Chapter 2 ("The Negativity Instinct") — why we think the world is getting worse when the data says otherwise; the secret silent miracle of human progress.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Amartya Sen, <em>Development as Freedom</em>, Chapter 2 (selections) — development is not just income growth: it is the expansion of real freedoms. What does this mean for how we measure progress?</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Sep 10</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title"><a href="session-notes/session04-great-enrichment.html">Session 4 · The Great Enrichment: Capitalism and Transformation</a></div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Simon D. Halliday &amp; Luz Marina Arias, <em>Understanding Our Economy</em>, Chapter 3: "Our world transformed" (selections) — how capitalism's institutions produced extraordinary growth, and at what cost.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Mark Koyama &amp; Jared Rubin, <em>How the World Became Rich</em>, Chapter 1: "Why, When, and How Did the World Become Rich?" (~10 pp.) — why did sustained growth only begin in the last two centuries, and why did the Malthusian trap persist for so long before that?</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://newsletter.rootsofprogress.org/p/fish-in-water" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Jason Crawford, "Fish in Water," Chapter 1 of <em>The Techno-Humanist Manifesto</em> (2024, ~2,500 words)</a> — industrial civilization has become invisible to us precisely because it has solved so many problems. A short provocation on why we take prosperity for granted, and why that matters.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Joseph A. Schumpeter, <em>Capitalism, Socialism and Democracy</em>, Chapter 7: "The Process of Creative Destruction" (1942, ~10 pp.) — the canonical source. Capitalism, Schumpeter argues, is not primarily about price competition but about the "gale of creative destruction" — new goods, methods, and organizational forms that ceaselessly displace the old. The chapter that gave the concept its name.</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span><span class="tag tag--accent">UOE Ch. 3</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Module 2 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section id="module-2">
  <div class="container">
    <div class="syllabus-module">
      <div class="syllabus-module__header">
        <span class="syllabus-module__num">Module 2</span>
        <h2 class="syllabus-module__title">Institutions &amp; Innovation</h2>
      </div>
      <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--ink-mid); margin-bottom: var(--sp-lg); max-width: 600px;">
        Progress does not happen in a vacuum. Institutions — the rules, norms, and organizations
        that structure economic life — shape who innovates, who benefits, and who is left out.
      </p>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Sep 15</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title"><a href="session-notes/session05-science-public-good.html">Session 5 · Science as a Public Good</a></div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Richard R. Nelson, &#x201C;The Simple Economics of Basic Scientific Research,&#x201D; <em>Journal of Political Economy</em> 67, no. 3 (1959): 297&#x2013;306 &#x2014; the foundational public-goods-of-science argument: why markets underinvest in basic research, and what institutions fill the gap.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Vannevar Bush, <em>Science, The Endless Frontier</em> (1945), transmittal letter + Chapter 3 (~8 pp.) &#x2014; the report that shaped US science policy for a generation. Why should government fund basic research? <a href="https://www.nsf.gov/od/lpa/nsf50/vbush1945.htm" target="_blank" rel="noopener">[free at nsf.gov]</a></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Partha Dasgupta and Paul A. David, &#x201C;Toward a New Economics of Science,&#x201D; <em>Research Policy</em> 23, no. 5 (1994): 487&#x2013;521 &#x2014; the fullest economic framework for scientific norms and open science. Read pp. 487&#x2013;497 if you read nothing else.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Paul M. Romer, &#x201C;The Origins of Endogenous Growth,&#x201D; <em>Journal of Economic Perspectives</em> 8, no. 1 (1994): 3&#x2013;22 &#x2014; accessible account of why non-rival ideas drive long-run growth; connects Nelson to growth theory.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Michael Polanyi, &#x201C;The Republic of Science,&#x201D; <em>Minerva</em> 1, no. 1 (1962): 54&#x2013;73 &#x2014; science as spontaneous order, analogous to a market. The philosophical complement to Nelson. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[PDF via library]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://experimentalhistory.substack.com/p/the-rise-and-fall-of-peer-review" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Adam Mastroianni, &#x201C;The Rise and Fall of Peer Review,&#x201D; <em>Experimental History</em> (2022)</a> &#x2014; does scientific self-governance actually work? A short, provocative read for students who want a contemporary angle.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Daniel Gross &amp; Bhaven Sampat, &#x201C;America, Jump-Started: World War II R&amp;D and the Takeoff of the U.S. Innovation System,&#x201D; <em>American Economic Review</em> 113, no. 7 (2023): 1737&#x2013;1781 &#x2014; how the wartime science mobilisation of 1940&#x2013;45 permanently shifted the US innovation system toward basic research and university-industry linkages. Context for why Bush&#x2019;s 1945 report landed when it did. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[via library]</span></li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Sep 17</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 6 · Innovation, Incentives, and Technology</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Simon D. Halliday &amp; Eric Bottorff, <em>Understanding Our Economy</em>, Chapter 4: "Innovation, incentives, and technology" (selections) — how do incentives shape the direction and pace of innovation?</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.20180338" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Nicholas Bloom, Charles I. Jones, John Van Reenen &amp; Michael Webb, "Are Ideas Getting Harder to Find?" <em>American Economic Review</em> 110, no. 4 (2020): 1104–1144</a> — read the introduction and conclusion only (~8 pp.). The central finding is striking and completely accessible: the number of researchers required to sustain Moore's Law has grown 18-fold since the early 1970s. Ideas are genuinely getting harder to find even as R&amp;D inputs soar — the empirical backbone for why innovation institutions and incentive structures matter so much.</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span><span class="tag tag--accent">UOE Ch. 4</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Sep 22</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 7 · Cities 1: Renaissance Florence and the Geography of Ideas</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta"><span style="color: var(--accent);">Field visit: Walters Art Museum, Mount Vernon — Renaissance collection.</span>
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Ada Palmer, excerpts from <em>Inventing the Renaissance</em> (2025) — what made Florence a crucible of innovation?</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://web.archive.org/web/https://www.vox.com/2016/5/18/11691818/barone-mocetti-florence" target="_blank" rel="noopener">"Florence's wealthiest families in 1427 are still the wealthiest today," <em>Vox</em> (May 18, 2016)</a> — an accessible account of Barone and Mocetti's finding that Florentine surnames linked to wealthy households in the 1427 <em>catasto</em> are still associated with high earnings today; a vivid illustration of how deep and durable economic status can be. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[archived version]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Dan Chiasson, "Man of the House" (<em>New Yorker</em>, Apr 29 2019) on the Bauhaus — a counterpoint on design, craft, and collective creativity.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://academic.oup.com/restud/article-abstract/88/4/1863/5958123" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Guglielmo Barone &amp; Sauro Mocetti, "Intergenerational Mobility in the Very Long Run: Florence 1427–2011," <em>Review of Economic Studies</em> 88, no. 4 (2021): 1863–1891</a> — matches Florentine <em>catasto</em> records from 1427 to modern tax data by surname; finds that earnings rank persists across 600 years at a rate comparable to single-generation estimates, suggesting economic status is far more entrenched than cross-generational studies typically show. The source behind the Vox piece. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[abstract; PDF via library]</span></li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span><span class="tag tag--accent">Walters Art Museum visit</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Sep 24</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 8 · Cities 2: Silicon Valley and Economies of Agglomeration</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://geekway.substack.com/p/a-visualization-of-europes-non-bubbly" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Andrew McAfee, &#x201C;A Visualization of Europe&#x2019;s Non-Bubbly Economy,&#x201D; <em>The Geek Way</em> (December 2024, ~1,200 words)</a> &#x2014; two bubble charts that make the US&#x2013;Europe innovation gap visceral: Europe has produced 14 from-scratch public companies worth over $10B in the past fifty years; the US has produced hundreds, collectively worth almost $30 trillion. McAfee draws on the Draghi report to diagnose why &#x2014; fragmented markets, restrictive regulation, and a VC ecosystem that lags the US by a factor of five at every funding stage. The sharpest short answer to &#x201C;why Silicon Valley?&#x201D; and &#x201C;why not Europe?&#x201D; <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[free, Substack]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> AnnaLee Saxenian, <em>Regional Advantage: Culture and Competition in Silicon Valley and Route 128</em>, Introduction (8 pp., 1996) &#x2014; explains why Silicon Valley recovered from the crises of the early 1980s while Route 128 did not. Saxenian's answer is institutional: Silicon Valley had open networks and fluid labor markets; Route 128 had hierarchical, self-contained firms. The Introduction is enough to grasp both the empirical puzzle and the core thesis. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[PDF on Canvas]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Sebastian Mallaby, <em>The Power Law: Venture Capital and the Making of the New Future</em>, Prologue (~15 pp., 2022) &#x2014; how did venture capital as a financing form emerge in California, and why has it proved so hard to replicate elsewhere? Mallaby traces the origins to a small group of investors who chose equity over fees &#x2014; a structural choice that, compounded over decades, produced the funding ecosystem McAfee&#x2019;s charts make visible. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[PDF via library]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Mario Draghi, <em>The Future of European Competitiveness</em> (European Commission, September 2024), pp. 1&#x2013;15 (Part A executive summary) &#x2014; the report McAfee is reacting to. Draghi argues Europe faces an &#x201C;existential crisis&#x201D; driven by falling productivity growth, underinvestment in deep tech, and regulatory fragmentation. The headline statistics &#x2014; no from-scratch EU company worth over &#x20AC;100B in fifty years; VC funding lagging the US at every stage by roughly 5&#xD7; &#x2014; are all here in primary form. Read pp. 1&#x2013;15 for the diagnosis; the rest is policy prescription. <a href="https://commission.europa.eu/document/download/97e481fd-2dc3-412d-be4c-f152a8232961_en?filename=The%20future%20of%20European%20competitiveness%20_%20A%20competitiveness%20strategy%20for%20Europe.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener">[PDF, free]</a></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://www.technologyreview.com/2015/08/18/10816/the-past-and-future-of-kendall-square/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Michael Blanding, &#x201C;The Past and Future of Kendall Square,&#x201D; <em>MIT Technology Review</em> (August 18, 2015)</a> &#x2014; a five-act history of how an abandoned industrial wasteland became what the Boston Consulting Group called &#x201C;the most innovative square mile on Earth.&#x201D; Covers the role of MIT lab spinouts (Biogen, Genzyme), the arrival of Big Pharma, and the deliberate curation of co-location as an innovation strategy. A Cambridge counterpart to the Silicon Valley story &#x2014; and a vivid illustration of what Saxenian&#x2019;s network-based model looks like in life sciences. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[free]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/02/24/business/engine-mit-is-revving-up-potential-tough-tech/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Scott Kirsner, &#x201C;The Engine at MIT is revving up the potential of &#x2018;tough tech,&#x2019;&#x201D; <em>Boston Globe</em> (February 24, 2020)</a> &#x2014; profiles MIT&#x2019;s tough-tech accelerator, which funds companies whose science is too capital-intensive and slow-moving for standard VC &#x2014; fusion energy, green steel, grid-scale batteries. Companies like Commonwealth Fusion Systems were born here. Connects Mallaby&#x2019;s VC origin story to the question of what happens to breakthroughs that don&#x2019;t fit the standard venture model. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[may require Globe subscription or library access]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://news.mit.edu/2021/MIT-CFS-major-advance-toward-fusion-energy-0908" target="_blank" rel="noopener">David Chandler, &#x201C;MIT-designed project achieves major advance toward fusion energy,&#x201D; <em>MIT News</em> (September 8, 2021)</a> &#x2014; the moment Commonwealth Fusion Systems and MIT&#x2019;s Plasma Science and Fusion Center demonstrated a 20-tesla superconducting magnet &#x2014; a world record, and the critical milestone on the path to SPARC, their planned fusion demonstration reactor. A concrete example of how a research university spinout can move from a class project to a world-changing engineering milestone &#x2014; and how that process looks nothing like a standard tech startup. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[free]</span></li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">Silicon Valley exists for reasons &#x2014; and so does the gap between it and everywhere else. McAfee makes the US&#x2013;Europe comparison impossible to dismiss; Saxenian and Moretti explain the agglomeration economics underneath it.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span><span class="tag tag--accent">Baltimore Video due</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Sep 29</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 9 · Cities 3: Baltimore — Progress, Decline, and Video Screenings</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Reading TBD — a short piece on Baltimore's industrial history, deindustrialization, and contemporary reinvention. Students watch their Baltimore videos <em>before</em> class (with a brief written reflection due that morning) and bring a discussion question. Class opens with the Baltimore reading, then moves to group video showcase and discussion.
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span><span class="tag tag--accent">Video reflection due</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Oct 1</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 10 · Liberty, Property Rights, and the Conditions for Progress</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://www.econlib.org/library/Essays/hykKnw.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">F. A. Hayek, "The Use of Knowledge in Society," <em>American Economic Review</em> 35, no. 4 (1945): 519–530</a> — the canonical argument that market prices aggregate dispersed private knowledge that no central planner can replicate. Why private property and decentralized decision-making are epistemologically necessary, not just incentive-compatible.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Reading TBD — an institutional perspective on why institutions, not just prices, shape who benefits from liberty. Options: Douglass North, <em>Institutions, Institutional Change and Economic Performance</em>, Ch. 1 (1990); Deirdre McCloskey, "The Formula for a Richer World? Equality, Liberty, Justice," <em>JEP</em> (2019).</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">We have seen what capitalism produced (S4) and how innovation incentives work (S6). This session asks what the prior conditions were — the institutional and political foundations without which those mechanisms cannot function.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Oct 6</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 11 · Escaping the Poverty Trap</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Yuen Yuen Ang, <em>How China Escaped the Poverty Trap</em>, Chapter 2 — how do poor societies bootstrap growth, and what does this mean for the role of the state?</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Module 3 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section id="module-3" style="background: var(--bg-alt);">
  <div class="container">
    <div class="syllabus-module">
      <div class="syllabus-module__header">
        <span class="syllabus-module__num">Module 3</span>
        <h2 class="syllabus-module__title">Who Benefits?</h2>
      </div>
      <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--ink-mid); margin-bottom: var(--sp-lg); max-width: 600px;">
        Progress is uneven. We look at both how much has improved — especially for groups historically
        excluded from prosperity — and where serious gaps remain. Income, wealth, representation,
        health: the picture is complicated, and the data matters.
      </p>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Oct 8</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 12 · Inequality, Mobility, and the Geography of Opportunity</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Simon D. Halliday, <em>Understanding Our Economy</em>, Chapter 5: "Inequality, fairness, and the institutions that shape them" (selections) — income and wealth inequality across countries; intergenerational and categorical inequality; the Gini coefficient; experimental evidence on fairness preferences; the efficiency-fairness tradeoff.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Raj Chetty &amp; Nathaniel Hendren, "The Impacts of Neighborhoods on Intergenerational Mobility I: Childhood Exposure Effects," <em>Quarterly Journal of Economics</em> 133, no. 3 (2018): 1107–1162 — read the Introduction and Conclusion only (~8 pp.). The evidence that where you grow up causally shapes where you end up: a natural experiment using families who move across counties.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">UOE Ch. 5 gives the conceptual infrastructure — how we measure and think about inequality, and what makes it persist. Chetty &amp; Hendren show the mechanism at work: neighborhood matters causally, not just correlationally.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span><span class="tag tag--accent">UOE Ch. 5</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Oct 13</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 13 · Race and Progress in America</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Isabel Wilkerson, <em>The Warmth of Other Suns</em>, Prologue (~10 pp.) — three Black Americans who joined the Great Migration, told as an act of economic agency and hope.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Eric Bottorff, Trevon Logan &amp; Suresh Naidu, <a href="https://books.core-econ.org/insights/persistent-racial-inequality-in-the-united-states/00-highlights.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">"Persistent Racial Inequality in the United States," <em>CORE Insights</em> (2022)</a> — the mechanisms of segregation, discrimination, and political inequality that have slowed convergence.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">Together: what did Black progress look like in motion, and why has it remained incomplete?</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Oct 15</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 14 · Baltimore: Industrial History and the Limits of Progress</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <strong>Field visit — Baltimore Museum of Industry (BMI).</strong>
            Reading TBD — background on Baltimore's industrial and labor history.
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">BMI field visit</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Oct 20</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 15 · Contemporary Barriers to Progress: Why We&#x2019;ve Stopped Building</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://www.theatlantic.com/science/archive/2019/07/we-need-new-science-progress/594946/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Patrick Collison &amp; Tyler Cowen, "We Need a New Science of Progress," <em>The Atlantic</em> (July 2019, ~1,500 words)</a> — the short manifesto that launched Progress Studies as a field. Why has no academic discipline made the causes of progress its central object of inquiry?</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Ezra Klein &amp; Derek Thompson, <em>Abundance</em>, Chapter 1: "The Politics of More" (2025, ~25 pp.) — the political economy of why America has stopped building: housing, clean energy, infrastructure. The argument that progressive politics has become entangled with a politics of restriction.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Brian Potter, <em>The Origins of Efficiency</em> (Stripe Press, 2025) — selections from the chapters on housing and construction (~20 pp.). Potter analyses why the production process in construction has not seen the efficiency gains of steel or semiconductors. A complementary, production-side analysis of the same barriers Klein &amp; Thompson identify from the political side.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">The course has asked why progress happened. This session asks why, in some domains and in some countries, it seems to have slowed — and what structural, political, and behavioural obstacles have replaced the old material ones.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Oct 22</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 16 · Progress and the Labor Market: Work, Wages, and What Has Changed</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Eric Bottorff &amp; Simon D. Halliday, <em>Understanding Our Economy</em>, Chapter 10: "Work, Pay, and Power" (selections) — how labor markets determine wages, what has changed in the structure of work since 1980, and why some workers gained while others did not.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/jep.29.3.3" target="_blank" rel="noopener">David H. Autor, "Why Are There Still So Many Jobs? The History and Future of Workplace Automation," <em>Journal of Economic Perspectives</em> 29, no. 3 (2015): 3–30</a> — automation substitutes for routine tasks but complements human judgment, adaptability, and problem-solving. The wage story is polarization, not elimination. The most-assigned piece in undergraduate labor economics for exactly this reason. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[free download from AEA]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://doi.org/10.1162/daed_a_01915" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Erik Brynjolfsson, "The Turing Trap: The Promise &amp; Peril of Human-Like Artificial Intelligence," <em>Daedalus</em> 151, no. 2 (2022): 272–287</a> — the question isn't whether AI will transform work: it's that we're choosing to build AI that replaces humans rather than augments them, and that choice is driven by incentives and politics, not technical necessity. The deeper intuition-disruptor for students who've already absorbed the Autor framing. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[JHU Daedalus access via library]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://doi.org/10.1126/science.abl4476" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Kuan-hao Park et al., "A Causal Test of the Strength of Weak Ties," <em>Science</em> 377, no. 6612 (2022): 1304–1310</a> — a large-scale natural experiment on LinkedIn showing that weak ties (acquaintances rather than close friends) are causally responsible for job mobility and wage gains. The classic Granovetter hypothesis, tested at scale.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">How do workers find jobs, and what determines wages? From the structure of labor markets to the networks that shape opportunity.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Module 4 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section id="module-4">
  <div class="container">
    <div class="syllabus-module">
      <div class="syllabus-module__header">
        <span class="syllabus-module__num">Module 4</span>
        <h2 class="syllabus-module__title">Ambition &amp; Agency</h2>
      </div>
      <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--ink-mid); margin-bottom: var(--sp-lg); max-width: 600px;">
        Does progress flow from exceptional individuals or inevitable structural forces?
        What is the role of ambition, luck, and the systems that enable or suppress it?
      </p>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Oct 27</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 17 · Great Men, Great Women, and Inevitable Inventions</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Thomas Carlyle, <a href="https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1091" target="_blank" rel="noopener">"The Hero as Man of Letters" (extract from <em>On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History</em>, 1841)</a> — the philosophical case for exceptional individuals as the engine of history.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Mark Koyama &amp; Jared Rubin, <em>How the World Became Rich</em>, Chapter 8: "Britain's Industrial Revolution" (selections, ~15 pp.) — why did sustained technological change first occur in Britain when it did, and not in the Dutch Republic, which shared so many of the same features?</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Benjamin F. Jones, &#x201C;The Burden of Knowledge and the &#x2018;Death of the Renaissance Man&#x2019;: Is Innovation Getting Harder?&#x201D; <em>Review of Economic Studies</em> 76, no. 1 (2009): 283&#x2013;317 &#x2014; innovators today must absorb far more prior knowledge before reaching the frontier; the result is longer training, narrower specialisation, and more teamwork. A structural argument that the age of the lone genius is ending &#x2014; not because people are less ambitious, but because knowledge has compounded. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[PDF via library]</span></li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">Together: was Watt inevitable?</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Oct 29</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 18 · Ambition</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta"><ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> William J. Baumol, &#x201C;Entrepreneurship: Productive, Unproductive, and Destructive,&#x201D; <em>Journal of Political Economy</em> 98, no. 5 (1990): 893&#x2013;921 &#x2014; the total supply of entrepreneurs may be roughly constant across societies; what varies &#x2014; enormously &#x2014; is how the rules of the game channel them toward innovation versus rent-seeking. A framework that explains why Rome produced lawyers and generals instead of engineers. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[PDF via library]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://hbr.org/2017/06/is-america-encouraging-the-wrong-kind-of-entrepreneurship" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Robert E. Litan &amp; Ian Hathaway, &#x201C;Is America Encouraging the Wrong Kind of Entrepreneurship?&#x201D; <em>Harvard Business Review</em> (June 2017)</a> &#x2014; written as a tribute to Baumol shortly after his death, this short piece asks whether current US incentives are steering entrepreneurs toward extractive activities (rent-seeking, financial engineering) rather than productive ones. A direct application of the Baumol framework to contemporary America.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Max Weber, <em>The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism</em>, Chapter 1: "Religious Affiliation and Social Stratification" (1905, ~20 pp.) &#x2014; the classic claim that cultural attitudes toward work, thrift, and calling shaped the emergence of capitalism in Protestant societies. Read as a foil to Baumol: are entrepreneurial energies shaped by institutions alone, or also by culture and belief?</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> Samuel Bazzi, Martin Fiszbein &amp; Mesay Gebresilasse, &#x201C;Frontier Culture: The Roots and Persistence of &#x2018;Rugged Individualism&#x2019; in the United States,&#x201D; <em>Econometrica</em> 88, no. 6 (2020): 2329&#x2013;2368 &#x2014; read the introduction and conclusion only (~8 pp.). Uses the historical westward movement of the frontier to show that individualistic, anti-government attitudes persist to this day in counties that were once frontier territory. A striking empirical test of the Weber-style claim that history shapes economic culture.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">Together: what kind of entrepreneurship does this society actually reward &#x2014; and is that the kind that produces progress?</p></div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Nov 3</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 19 · Guest: Political Economy of Reform</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Guest speaker: Professor Steve Teles (Hopkins).
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://www.nationalaffairs.com/publications/detail/kludgeocracy-in-america" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Steven Teles, &#x201C;Kludgeocracy in America,&#x201D; <em>National Affairs</em> (Fall 2013)</a> &#x2014; America&#x2019;s patchwork of overlapping programmes and indirect policy instruments is not an accident but a structural feature of its political system. Teles coins &#x201C;kludgeocracy&#x201D; to name a governing style that is simultaneously complex, inefficient, and redistributive &#x2014; upward. Essential background for understanding why reform is so hard.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://www.niskanencenter.org/the-rise-of-the-abundance-faction/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Robert Saldin &amp; Steven Teles, &#x201C;The Rise of the Abundance Faction,&#x201D; Niskanen Center (June 2024)</a> &#x2014; argues that a cross-cutting &#x201C;Abundance Faction&#x201D; &#x2014; YIMBY activists, climate builders, healthcare reformers &#x2014; is emerging as a new political force in American politics, modelled on the Progressive Era coalition. The political complement to Kludgeocracy&#x2019;s diagnosis.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> William J. Baumol &amp; William G. Bowen, &#x201C;On the Performing Arts: The Anatomy of Their Economic Problems,&#x201D; <em>American Economic Review</em> 55, no. 1/2 (1965): 495&#x2013;502 &#x2014; the original &#x201C;cost disease&#x201D; paper. Live performance cannot substitute capital for labor the way manufacturing can, so its costs rise relentlessly relative to the rest of the economy. Wide implications for education, healthcare, and government services.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">Kludgeocracy names the disease; the Abundance Faction proposes the cure. Come ready to push on both: is the diagnosis right? Is the proposed coalition realistic?</p></div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">Guest speaker</span><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Nov 5</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 20 · Top-Down Innovation: The State, the Market, and Who Decides</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://blog.benjaminreinhardt.com/wddw" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Ben Reinhardt, &#x201C;Why Does DARPA Work?&#x201D; (~20 pp.)</a> &#x2014; a careful structural analysis of what makes DARPA, uniquely among government agencies, a reliable producer of breakthrough innovation: programme managers with autonomy, short time horizons, and no bureaucratic overhead. The template against which all government innovation is measured.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/jep.31.1.67" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Heidi Williams, &#x201C;How Do Patents Affect Research Investments?&#x201D; <em>Journal of Economic Perspectives</em> 31, no. 1 (2017): 67&#x2013;92</a> &#x2014; a survey of the empirical evidence on whether and when intellectual property rights increase or decrease downstream innovation. Williams&#x2019;s own work on the human genome showed that private IP rights over mapped gene sequences reduced subsequent research on those genes by ~30% &#x2014; a striking finding about how the design of property rights, not just their existence, shapes what gets discovered.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://www.statecraft.pub/p/how-to-replicate-operation-warp-speed" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Santi Ruiz, &#x201C;How to Replicate Operation Warp Speed,&#x201D; <em>Statecraft</em> (January 2024)</a> &#x2014; a Q&amp;A with former HHS Secretary Alex Azar on what actually made OWS work: unlimited crisis funding, the right people, an unprecedented DOD partnership, and brute-force execution of a problem that was already solved scientifically. Azar is candid about why it can&#x2019;t be easily replicated &#x2014; &#x201C;It had to be a miracle. We got a miracle&#x201D; &#x2014; and what that implies for how we design government innovation programs going forward.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">Case studies: DARPA, Operation Warp Speed, Bell Labs, the internet. What makes government-directed innovation succeed or fail &#x2014; and how does it interact with the patent system that governs what the private sector investigates?</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Nov 10</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 21 · Guest: Contemporary Science Policy</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Guest speaker from the Institute for Progress (IFP) &#x2014; Heidi Williams (Director of Science Policy, IFP; Professor of Economics, Dartmouth) or Santi Ruiz (IFP Senior Editor, <em>Statecraft</em>), subject to availability.
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://ifp.org/to-speed-scientific-progress-do-away-with-funding-delays/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Heidi Williams, &#x201C;Funding Delays Are Slowing Scientific Progress,&#x201D; Institute for Progress (August 2023, ~1,500 words)</a> &#x2014; scientists wait on average 20 months between submitting a grant and receiving funding. Williams argues this delay is not an administrative inevitability but a policy choice with measurable costs to research output &#x2014; and that fixing it is low-hanging fruit.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://ifp.org/to-speed-up-scientific-progress-we-need-to-understand-science-policy/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Matt Clancy, Dan Correa, Jordan Dworkin, Paul Niehaus, Caleb Watney &amp; Heidi Williams, &#x201C;To Speed Up Scientific Progress, We Need to Understand Science Policy,&#x201D; Institute for Progress (September 2023)</a> &#x2014; a short manifesto arguing that the gap between science policy research and actual policy-making is itself a barrier to progress, and proposing three bridges to close it.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">IFP works at the intersection of academic research and policy practice. Come with a specific claim from the Williams reading you want to push back on &#x2014; or a funding delay story of your own.</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">Guest speaker</span><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Module 5 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section id="module-5" style="background: var(--bg-alt);">
  <div class="container">
    <div class="syllabus-module">
      <div class="syllabus-module__header">
        <span class="syllabus-module__num">Module 5</span>
        <h2 class="syllabus-module__title">Limits &amp; Risks</h2>
      </div>
      <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--ink-mid); margin-bottom: var(--sp-lg); max-width: 600px;">
        Progress can stall, reverse, or create new problems faster than it solves old ones.
        Climate change, pandemic risk, and technological disruption test whether the optimist story holds.
      </p>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Nov 12</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 22 · Environmental Progress: Can We Fix What We Broke?</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Hannah Ritchie, <em>Not the End of the World</em> (2024), Chapter 1: "Air Pollution" (~35 pp.) — the evidence for environmental progress, and where it falls short.</li>
            </ul>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Nov 17</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 23 · When Progress Ends — and What We Must Maintain</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://www.theatlantic.com/magazine/archive/2023/01/science-technology-vaccine-invention-history/672227/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Derek Thompson, "Why the Age of American Progress Ended," <em>The Atlantic</em> (Jan/Feb 2023)</a> — the case that innovation has slowed and what might explain it. <span style="color:var(--ink-light);">[free, may ask you to create an account]</span></li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> <a href="https://aeon.co/essays/innovation-is-overvalued-maintenance-often-matters-more" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Andrew Russell &amp; Lee Vinsel, "Hail the Maintainers," <em>Aeon</em> (2016, ~2,900 words)</a> — the counterargument: we celebrated disruption while neglecting the work of keeping things running.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://newsletter.rootsofprogress.org/p/solutionism-part-1" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Jason Crawford, "Solutionism, Part 1," Chapter 5 of <em>The Techno-Humanist Manifesto</em> (2025, ~3,000 words)</a> — from Malthus to the Green Revolution: why the right response to a real crisis is neither complacency nor despair, but active problem-solving. A counterpoint to Thompson.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">Is progress failing because we stopped inventing, or because we stopped maintaining? And when it does seem to stall — what does history say we should do about it?</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Nov 19</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 24 · Catastrophic Risk: Pandemic, AI, and Existential Threats</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Reading TBD — options: Nick Bostrom excerpt; post-pandemic review article; AI safety short reading. Discussion: what obligations does anticipated risk create?</div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag">In-class response</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row" style="opacity: 0.7;">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Nov 24</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title" style="color: var(--ink-light); font-style: italic;">No class — Thanksgiving week</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta" style="color: var(--ink-light);">No classes this week (Tuesday and Thursday both cancelled).</div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row" style="opacity: 0.7;">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Nov 26</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title" style="color: var(--ink-light); font-style: italic;">No class — Thanksgiving</div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Module 6 ────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section id="module-6">
  <div class="container">
    <div class="syllabus-module">
      <div class="syllabus-module__header">
        <span class="syllabus-module__num">Module 6</span>
        <h2 class="syllabus-module__title">Looking Forward</h2>
      </div>
      <p style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.9rem; color: var(--ink-mid); margin-bottom: var(--sp-lg); max-width: 600px;">
        Students present their Data Projects and the seminar closes with reflection
        and celebration. What has changed in your thinking since September 1?
      </p>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Dec 1</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 25 · The Future of Progress</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">
            <ul class="reading-list">
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Ted Chiang, "Exhalation" (2019, ~6,000 words) — a meditation on entropy, memory, and what it means to understand. Read slowly. Bring one sentence that you think is about progress, even though the story never uses that word.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--required">Required</span> Reading TBD — a forward-looking essay or further chapter from Jason Crawford&#x2019;s <em>The Techno-Humanist Manifesto</em> on what a society that takes progress seriously would look like.</li>
              <li><span class="tag tag--recommended">Recommended</span> <a href="https://www.cold-takes.com/most-important-century/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Holden Karnofsky, "The Most Important Century" (Cold Takes, 2021)</a> — a careful case that we may be living in the most consequential period in human history, and what that implies for how we think about the future.</li>
            </ul>
            <p class="reading-note">The course ends by looking forward. Where does progress go from here — and what kind of people do we need to be to keep it going?</p>
          </div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">Data project written report due</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Dec 3</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 26 · Data Project Presentations (Part 1)</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Six presentations, ~10 minutes each + Q&amp;A. No advance reading assigned.</div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">Data project presentations</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Tue Dec 8</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 27 · Data Project Presentations (Part 2)</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Six presentations, ~10 minutes each + Q&amp;A. No advance reading assigned.</div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">Data project presentations</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="session-row">
        <div class="session-row__date">Thu Dec 10</div>
        <div class="session-row__body">
          <div class="session-row__title">Session 28 · Closing: Celebration and Final Questions</div>
          <div class="session-row__meta">Students share closing reflections (revisiting Week 1's opening prompt). The seminar ends with open questions students have brought from across the semester — the things they still want to know. Cookies may be involved.</div>
          <div class="session-row__tags"><span class="tag tag--accent">Closing reflection due</span></div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Pedagogy Note ───────────────────────────────────────────── -->
<section style="background: var(--bg-alt);">
  <div class="container--text">
    <h2>A note on AI use</h2>
    <p>
      I will use, and have used, AI in this course. AI helps me design the website, create assignments,
      write session notes, find data, think about readings in ways opposed to the way I think about them,
      and in many other ways that are hard to describe and name. Moreover, given the current milieu,
      I think it would be unethical for me not to use AI to provide you with the best course I possibly
      can, while also retaining strong intellectual ownership over the course's contents and its
      trajectory. Similarly, I think it would be misguided for you not to use AI.
    </p>
    <p>
      At the same time, it is crucial that you nonetheless learn how to write well, to develop taste,
      to hone your intuition, to develop a strong bullshit detector. For all of these, you need to read
      without AI. You need to write without AI. You need to sit and pause and write and think and reflect
      alone. You need to develop practices for using AI wisely and for clarifying when its use is
      inappropriate for your own development as a thoughtful and moral person and citizen.
    </p>
    <p>
      A variety of pieces have informed what I think about this topic, and I have included some of them below.
    </p>
    <ul style="font-family: var(--font-sans); font-size: 0.875rem; line-height: 1.7; color: var(--ink-mid);">
      <li>Noy, Shakked, and Whitney Zhang. "<a href="https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.adh2586" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Experimental evidence on the productivity effects of generative artificial intelligence</a>." <em>Science</em> 381, no. 6654 (2023): 187–192.</li>
      <li>Mollick, Ethan, and Lilach Mollick. "<a href="https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4475995" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Assigning AI: Seven approaches for students, with prompts</a>." SSRN Working Paper, 2023.</li>
      <li>Lee, Hao-Ping (Hank), Advait Sarkar, Lev Tankelevitch, Ian Drosos, Sean Rintel, Richard Banks, and Nicholas Wilson. "<a href="https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/lee_2025_ai_critical_thinking_survey.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener">The Impact of Generative AI on Critical Thinking: Self-Reported Reductions in Cognitive Effort and Confidence Effects From a Survey of Knowledge Workers</a>." In <em>CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems</em> (CHI '25), April 26–May 1, 2025, Yokohama, Japan. ACM. DOI: 10.1145/3706598.3713778.</li>
      <li>Bjork, Robert A., and Elizabeth L. Bjork. "<a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jarmac.2020.09.003" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Desirable difficulties in theory and practice</a>." <em>Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition</em> 9, no. 4 (2020): 475–479.</li>
      <li>Kapur, Manu. "<a href="https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/07370000802212669" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Productive failure</a>." <em>Cognition and Instruction</em> 26, no. 3 (2008): 379–424.</li>
      <li>Ericsson, K. Anders, Ralf Th. Krampe, and Clemens Tesch-Römer. "<a href="https://gwern.net/doc/psychology/writing/1993-ericsson.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener">The role of deliberate practice in the acquisition of expert performance</a>." <em>Psychological Review</em> 100, no. 3 (1993): 363–406.</li>
      <li>Hambrick, David Z., Frederick L. Oswald, Erik M. Altmann, Elizabeth J. Meinz, Fernand Gobet, and Guillermo Campitelli. "<a href="https://gwern.net/doc/psychology/chess/2014-hambrick.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Deliberate practice: Is that all it takes to become an expert?</a>" <em>Intelligence</em> 45 (2014): 34–44.</li>
      <li>Branwen, Gwern. "<a href="https://gwern.net/spaced-repetition" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Spaced repetition for efficient learning</a>." gwern.net, 2009–2024.</li>
    </ul>
    <p>
      Consequently, I welcome and expect you to use AI tools thoughtfully in this seminar. Assignments
      are designed so that AI assistance produces better thinking, not a shortcut around it. Every major
      assignment includes an <strong>AI log</strong>: a brief account of how you used AI tools, what they
      produced, and how your own thinking differed from or built on the AI's output.
    </p>
    <p>
      The goal is not to police AI use but to make it visible, so that you can develop
      a critical relationship with these tools, understanding both what they can do and what they
      cannot replace.
    </p>
  </div>
</section>

<!-- ── Footer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->
```
